**Brandon Michael Vera** (born October 10, 1977), or also known by his nickname "The Truth", is a Filipino and American retired [mixed martial artist](/wiki/Mixed_martial_artist "Mixed martial artist") where he was the inaugural [ONE Heavyweight Champion](/wiki/List_of_ONE_Championship_champions#Heavyweight_World_Championship "List of ONE Championship champions") and the [2005 WEC Heavyweight Grand Prix Champion](/wiki/List_of_WEC_champions#Tournament_Winners "List of WEC champions"). A professional competitor from 2002 until 2022, he competed in the [Light Heavyweight](/wiki/Light_Heavyweight_(MMA) "Light Heavyweight (MMA)") and [Heavyweight](/wiki/Heavyweight_(MMA) "Heavyweight (MMA)") divisions of [ONE Championship](/wiki/ONE_Championship "ONE Championship"), [World Extreme Cagefighting](/wiki/World_Extreme_Cagefighting "World Extreme Cagefighting") (WEC) and [Ultimate Fighting Championship](/wiki/Ultimate_Fighting_Championship "Ultimate Fighting Championship") (UFC).

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brandon_Vera&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Brandon Michael Vera grew up in a house of seven boys and three girls. He was born to a [Filipino](/wiki/Filipinos "Filipinos") father, Ernesto, and an American mother of [Italian descent](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans"), but raised by his Filipino stepmother, Amelia. He also has two other brothers and another sister outside of the family in which he was raised and they were occasionally involved in his life. Vera was born and raised in [Norfolk, Virginia](/wiki/Norfolk,_Virginia "Norfolk, Virginia"),[[4]](#cite_note-sdreader-5) and learned Filipino culture growing up; one of the rules in the family was that everyone had to speak [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language") (which Vera learned from his grandmother who spoke no English), and if any of the children were caught speaking English in the house, Vera's father would punish them.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

Vera attended Lake Taylor High School where he excelled in [wrestling](/wiki/Scholastic_wrestling "Scholastic wrestling") and earned a four-year athletic scholarship to [Old Dominion University](/wiki/Old_Dominion_University "Old Dominion University"). However, he dropped out of Old Dominion after a year and a half when he felt college was not for him, and enlisted himself in the [United States Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force"). In the Air Force, Vera joined the wrestling team and trained at the United States [Olympic Training Center](/wiki/United_States_Olympic_Training_Center "United States Olympic Training Center") in [Colorado Springs](/wiki/Colorado_Springs,_Colorado "Colorado Springs, Colorado"), [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"). His military wrestling career was cut short in 1999 when he tore ligaments in his right elbow. [Arthroscopic](/wiki/Arthroscopy "Arthroscopy") surgery repaired the ligaments, but he had nerve damage from the experience, causing him to be unable to use his right arm. He was released from the Air Force on a medical discharge.

Following his discharge from the Air Force, Vera returned to Virginia, where he steadily rehabilitated his arm, and eventually was fit enough to enter the Grapplers Quest submission wrestling competitions on the East Coast. There, his solitary training methods (he did not belong to a camp and trained and cut weight on his own) caught the attention of [Lloyd Irvin](/wiki/Lloyd_Irvin "Lloyd Irvin"), a [Brazilian jiu-jitsu](/wiki/Brazilian_jiu-jitsu "Brazilian jiu-jitsu") Black Belt and coach, who invited him to train with his school. At Irvin's school, he was introduced to mixed martial arts.[[4]](#cite_note-sdreader-5)

## Mixed martial arts career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brandon_Vera&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Mixed martial arts career")]

### Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brandon_Vera&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early career")]

Vera's first professional mixed martial arts bout was on July 6, 2002, while training under [Lloyd Irvin](/wiki/Lloyd_Irvin "Lloyd Irvin"). He won the fight against Adam Rivera via [TKO](/wiki/Technical_Knockout "Technical Knockout") in the first round. He fought and won another bout in 2004 before entering the [WEC 13 Heavyweight Tournament](/wiki/List_of_WEC_events "List of WEC events") in 2005, where he won two bouts in one night, including a bout against *[The Ultimate Fighter 2's](/wiki/The_Ultimate_Fighter_2 "The Ultimate Fighter 2")* [Mike Whitehead](/wiki/Mike_Whitehead "Mike Whitehead") in the final.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

Vera then moved to [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego"), [California](/wiki/California "California"), on December 31, 2003, to accept a training position with City Boxing. At City Boxing, Vera excelled as a trainer and was taken under the wing of owner Mark Dion, who became his manager and introduced him to [kickboxing](/wiki/Kickboxing "Kickboxing") great [Rob Kaman](/wiki/Rob_Kaman "Rob Kaman"). With Vera's success as a trainer and a mixed martial arts fighter, Dion gave Vera partial ownership of City Boxing.[[4]](#cite_note-sdreader-5)

### Ultimate Fighting Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brandon_Vera&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ultimate Fighting Championship")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/BrandonVera.png/250px-BrandonVera.png)](/wiki/File:BrandonVera.png)

Vera in 2007.

Vera made his UFC debut at *[Ultimate Fight Night 2](/wiki/UFC_Ultimate_Fight_Night_2 "UFC Ultimate Fight Night 2")* on October 3, 2005, against BJJ black belt [Fabiano Scherner](/wiki/Fabiano_Scherner "Fabiano Scherner"). Vera won the fight via KO due to knees from the clinch midway through the second round. Following the Scherner bout, he faced [Justin Eilers](/wiki/Justin_Eilers "Justin Eilers") at *[UFC 57](/wiki/UFC_57 "UFC 57")*, winning early in the first round by KO. At *[UFC 60](/wiki/UFC_60 "UFC 60")*, Vera defeated [Assuério Silva](/wiki/Assu%C3%A9rio_Silva "Assuério Silva") with a [guillotine choke](/wiki/Guillotine_choke "Guillotine choke") in the first round. On November 18, he stopped former heavyweight champion [Frank Mir](/wiki/Frank_Mir "Frank Mir") by TKO due to strikes in just 69 seconds at *[UFC 65](/wiki/UFC_65 "UFC 65")* in [Sacramento, California](/wiki/Sacramento,_California "Sacramento, California").

White had been telling the media prior to *[UFC 65](/wiki/UFC_65 "UFC 65")* that the winner of the Vera-Mir fight would face the winner of the [Tim Sylvia](/wiki/Tim_Sylvia "Tim Sylvia")-[Jeff Monson](/wiki/Jeff_Monson "Jeff Monson") bout, which was also being held that same night, for the championship. Vera's victory secured him a championship bout against then title-holder [Tim Sylvia](/wiki/Tim_Sylvia "Tim Sylvia"), but a contract dispute with the UFC forced him to be replaced by [Randy Couture](/wiki/Randy_Couture "Randy Couture").

In August, the UFC announced the "return" of Vera. His first fight was at *[UFC 77](/wiki/UFC_77 "UFC 77")* against Sylvia, who had recently lost the [UFC Heavyweight Championship](/wiki/UFC_Heavyweight_Championship "UFC Heavyweight Championship") to Couture.[[7]](#cite_note-8) Vera lost for the first time via unanimous decision. He also broke his left hand at 4:40 of the first round.

Vera had his second loss at *[UFC 85](/wiki/UFC_85 "UFC 85")* against Brazilian jiu-jitsu specialist [Fabrício Werdum](/wiki/Fabr%C3%ADcio_Werdum "Fabrício Werdum") via TKO. The fight was controversially stopped by referee Dan Miragliotta as Werdum mounted him and landed some ground and pound. After the stoppage, Vera was upset as he felt he was defending himself effectively.[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10)

After his two recent losses, Vera dropped down to the [Light Heavyweight](/wiki/Light_Heavyweight_(MMA) "Light Heavyweight (MMA)") division, facing IFL alum [Reese Andy](/wiki/Reese_Andy "Reese Andy") at *[UFC Fight Night 14](/wiki/UFC:_Silva_vs._Irvin "UFC: Silva vs. Irvin")* on July 19, 2008, on [Spike TV](/wiki/Spike_TV "Spike TV"). Vera defeated Andy via unanimous decision.

At [UFC 89](/wiki/UFC_89 "UFC 89"), Vera lost to [Keith Jardine](/wiki/Keith_Jardine "Keith Jardine") via a narrow split decision. Following the fight, Vera incurred criticism for his performance since his return to the UFC having been victorious in only one of his last four fights.

A more focused Vera appeared on the preliminary card of [UFC 96](/wiki/UFC_96 "UFC 96"). It was the first time Vera was not on the main card since he made his debut in the UFC. He had an impressive performance against Mike Patt, showing a more aggressive and intense striking approach and stopping him via TKO (leg kicks) in the second round.

Vera fought Polish fighter [Krzysztof Soszynski](/wiki/Krzysztof_Soszynski "Krzysztof Soszynski") at [UFC 102](/wiki/UFC_102 "UFC 102") after his original opponent, [Matt Hamill](/wiki/Matt_Hamill "Matt Hamill"), was forced to drop out due after suffering a meniscus tear while training.[[10]](#cite_note-11) Vera won via unanimous decision (30–27, 30–27, and 30–27).

Vera lost a close fight to [Randy Couture](/wiki/Randy_Couture "Randy Couture") via unanimous decision on November 14, 2009, at [UFC 105](/wiki/UFC_105 "UFC 105"), with the media comparing the decision to a previous UFC event (UFC 104 Machida-Rua) which prompted that MMA judging should be changed. Despite Vera significantly outstriking Couture and successfully defending numerous takedown attempts, judges awarded the victory to Couture. The verdict surprised many people; UFC commentator Joe Rogan was highly critical of the decision during the live event broadcast and in a post-fight interview, Randy Couture admitted that he didn't expect the decision to be made in his favor.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)

Vera faced [Jon Jones](/wiki/Jon_Jones "Jon Jones") on March 21, 2010, at [UFC Live: Vera vs. Jones](/wiki/UFC_Live:_Vera_vs._Jones "UFC Live: Vera vs. Jones") and lost via TKO in the first round after an elbow from Jones broke Vera's face in three places.

Vera was defeated by Brazilian [Thiago Silva](/wiki/Thiago_Silva_(fighter) "Thiago Silva (fighter)") via unanimous decision (30–26, 30–27, and 30–27) on January 1, 2011, at [UFC 125](/wiki/UFC_125 "UFC 125"). At the end of the third round, Vera stood up to reveal a badly broken nose.[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) Vera was released by the UFC with a 7–6 record in the organization.[[15]](#cite_note-16) However following the fight it was revealed that his opponent Thiago Silva had failed the post-fight drug test.[[16]](#cite_note-17) As a result of this, Vera was re-hired by the UFC and the result of the Silva fight was changed to a no contest, resulting in Vera's UFC record changing to 7–5–0–1.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

Vera defeated [Eliot Marshall](/wiki/Eliot_Marshall "Eliot Marshall") on October 29, 2011, at [UFC 137](/wiki/UFC_137 "UFC 137") via unanimous decision (29–28, 29–28, and 29–28).[[18]](#cite_note-19)

Vera was briefly linked to a rematch [Thiago Silva](/wiki/Thiago_Silva_(fighter) "Thiago Silva (fighter)") on May 15, 2012, at [UFC on Fuel TV: Korean Zombie vs. Poirier](/wiki/UFC_on_Fuel_TV:_Korean_Zombie_vs._Poirier "UFC on Fuel TV: Korean Zombie vs. Poirier").[[19]](#cite_note-20) However, Vera was forced out the bout with an injury.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

Vera was expected to face Australian [James Te-Huna](/wiki/James_Te-Huna "James Te-Huna") on July 11, 2012, at [UFC on Fuel TV: Munoz vs. Weidman](/wiki/UFC_on_Fuel_TV:_Munoz_vs._Weidman "UFC on Fuel TV: Munoz vs. Weidman").[[21]](#cite_note-cagepotato.com-22) However, Vera was pulled from the bout with Te-Huna to face [Maurício Rua](/wiki/Maur%C3%ADcio_Rua "Maurício Rua") on August 4, 2012, at [UFC on Fox: Shogun vs. Vera](/wiki/UFC_on_Fox:_Shogun_vs._Vera "UFC on Fox: Shogun vs. Vera").[[21]](#cite_note-cagepotato.com-22) Vera was defeated via TKO late in the fourth round. Vera showed great heart after being rocked early in the second round, but as both fighters showed early signs of fatigue in the late stages of the second round, Vera ultimately succumbed to Rua's power. This fight is widely considered the best performance of Vera's career, despite the loss.

Vera returned to the [Heavyweight](/wiki/Heavyweight_(MMA) "Heavyweight (MMA)") division after an absence of 5 years and faced [Ben Rothwell](/wiki/Ben_Rothwell "Ben Rothwell") at [UFC 164](/wiki/UFC_164 "UFC 164") on August 31, 2013.[[22]](#cite_note-23) After a competitive first and second round, Rothwell defeated Vera by TKO in the third round. Subsequent to the bout, Rothwell tested positive for elevated testosterone levels. The UFC suspended Rothwell for nine months, despite the Wisconsin commission only issuing him with an administrative warning.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

Vera was released from the UFC on June 17, 2014, ending his eight-year run with the promotion.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

### ONE Championship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brandon_Vera&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: ONE Championship")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Brandon_Vera_v._Mauro_Cerilli_Conquest_of_Champions.jpg/250px-Brandon_Vera_v._Mauro_Cerilli_Conquest_of_Champions.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brandon_Vera_v._Mauro_Cerilli_Conquest_of_Champions.jpg)

Vera (left) and Mauro Cerilli (right) in the "Conquest of Champions".

On July 13, 2014, it was confirmed that Vera had signed with [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore")-based promotion [ONE FC](/wiki/ONE_Championship "ONE Championship").[[25]](#cite_note-26) His debut was on December 5, 2014, against Ukrainian striker Igor Subora at [ONE Fighting Championship: Warrior's Way](/wiki/ONE_FC_Events#ONE_Fighting_Championship:_Warrior's_Way "ONE FC Events").[[26]](#cite_note-27) Vera won the fight via knockout after landing a counter straight left at 3:54 minutes in the first round.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

Vera was originally scheduled to face English kickboxing specialist [Chi Lewis-Parry](/wiki/Chi_Lewis-Parry "Chi Lewis-Parry") at [ONE Championship: Spirit of Champions](/wiki/ONE_FC_Events#ONE_Championship:_Spirit_of_Champions "ONE FC Events") on December 11, 2015 to crown the inaugural [ONE Heavyweight Champion](/wiki/List_of_ONE_Championship_champions#Heavyweight_World_Championship "List of ONE Championship champions"). However, he faced Taiwanese Paul Cheng instead, who replaced Parry on three days-notice after Parry failed to submit his medical records, did not show up for the pre-event press conference nor board his flight to Manila. Vera won the fight via knockout just twenty six seconds into the first round to win the championship.

After nearly a year away from the cage, Vera returned to face Japanese grappler Hideki Sekine at [ONE Championship: Age of Domination](/wiki/ONE_FC_Events#ONE_Championship:_Age_of_Domination "ONE FC Events") on December 2, 2016. He successfully defended his heavyweight title, winning by TKO in the first round.

Vera successfully defended his title via knockout in the first round against [Cage Warriors](/wiki/Cage_Warriors "Cage Warriors") Heavyweight Champion, Mauro Cerilli who was on his promotional debut at [ONE Championship: Conquest of Champions](/wiki/List_of_ONE_Championship_events "List of ONE Championship events") on November 23, 2018.[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32)

As the first fight of his new ten-bout contract with ONE, Vera challenged [Aung La Nsang](/wiki/Aung_La_Nsang "Aung La Nsang") for the ONE Light Heavyweight Championship at [ONE Championship: Century](/wiki/2019_in_ONE_Championship#ONE_Championship:_Century "2019 in ONE Championship") on October 13, 2019 in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), Japan, thus marking Vera's first fight in ONE Championship to take place outside of the Philippines.[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34) Vera lost by TKO in the second round.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

At [ONE Championship: Fire & Fury](/wiki/2020_in_ONE_Championship#ONE_Championship:_Fire_&_Fury "2020 in ONE Championship") on January 31, 2020, Vera announced that he would be defending his ONE Heavyweight Championship on May 29 in Manila, with an opponent yet to be announced.[[35]](#cite_note-36) It was later announced that he would be defending his ONE Heavyweight title against [Arjan Bhullar](/wiki/Arjan_Bhullar "Arjan Bhullar") at [ONE Infinity 2](/wiki/2020_in_ONE_Championship "2020 in ONE Championship").[[36]](#cite_note-37) Their fight was later postponed as a result of the [impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on sports](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_sports "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on sports").

Vera faced Bhullar at [ONE Championship: Dangal](/wiki/2021_in_ONE_Championship#ONE_Championship:_Dangal "2021 in ONE Championship") on May 15, 2021, with the fight taking place in [Kallang](/wiki/Kallang "Kallang"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore").[[37]](#cite_note-38) Vera lost to Bhullar by second-round technical knockout, ending his five-year reign as ONE Heavyweight World Champion.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

Vera faced [Amir Aliakbari](/wiki/Amir_Aliakbari "Amir Aliakbari") on December 3, 2022, at [ONE 164](/wiki/2022_in_ONE_Championship#ONE_164:_Pacio_vs._Brooks "2022 in ONE Championship").[[39]](#cite_note-40) He lost the fight via technical knockout in the first round and announced his retirement during the post-fight interview.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

## Post-MMA career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brandon_Vera&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Post-MMA career")]

After announcing his retirement in December 2022, Vera revealed that he signed a 10-movie deal as an actor in the Philippines.[[41]](#cite_note-42) Prior to this announcement, Vera already appeared in a couple of Filipino movies. In 2018, Vera co-starred with [Anne Curtis](/wiki/Anne_Curtis "Anne Curtis") in the Philippine action-thriller *[BuyBust](/wiki/BuyBust "BuyBust")*[[42]](#cite_note-43) and starred as the lead actor in a 2022 Filipino zombie movie called *Day Zero*.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brandon_Vera&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Vera was married to Kerry Kasik, a fellow former mixed martial artist who fought for the now-defunct [Strikeforce](/wiki/Strikeforce_(mixed_martial_arts) "Strikeforce (mixed martial arts)"). She was featured on the second season of [Oxygen's](/wiki/Oxygen_(TV_channel) "Oxygen (TV channel)") *[Fight Girls](/wiki/Fight_Girls "Fight Girls")*.[[44]](#cite_note-45) In September 2014, Kerry filed for divorce, which was finalized two years later.[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)

During the latter stages of his training for [UFC 89](/wiki/UFC_89 "UFC 89"), Vera was reportedly held at gun point by two men attempting to rob the house in which he was staying. Vera stated that the incident did not affect his performance against [Keith Jardine](/wiki/Keith_Jardine "Keith Jardine").[[47]](#cite_note-48)

Vera started dating Jessica Craven in 2016 and announced their engagement in April 2017.[[48]](#cite_note-49) They wed on April 20, 2018, in Guam.[[49]](#cite_note-50) Their son, Atreyu Timothy, was born on July 26, 2020.[[50]](#cite_note-51)

Vera has numerous tattoos, one of which is the Filipino scribe [Baybayin](/wiki/Baybayin "Baybayin") inked on his back, which reads *mundo* (earth), *hangin* (wind), *apoy* (fire) and *tubig* (water), clockwise.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

While staying in the Philippines, he trained Filipino actor [Richard Gutierrez](/wiki/Richard_Gutierrez "Richard Gutierrez") in martial arts and was given a role as an assassin for Philippine primetime television show, *[Kamandag](/wiki/Kamandag "Kamandag")* on the [GMA Network](/wiki/GMA_Network "GMA Network").[[52]](#cite_note-53)

In 2016, Vera revealed that he was relocating to the Philippines full time, to help grow the sport of mixed martial arts in his ancestral homeland.[[53]](#cite_note-54) He has been working on opening a branch of [Alliance MMA Gym](/wiki/Alliance_MMA_Gym "Alliance MMA Gym"). Vera holds dual citizenship: American (by *[jus solis](/wiki/Jus_solis "Jus solis")*) and Filipino (by *[jus sanguinis](/wiki/Jus_sanguinis "Jus sanguinis")*).
